title: Content-Based Filtering: Recommend by Similarity
concept: Content-based filtering recommends items by comparing their attributes directly to what you've liked in the past — matching movie genres, article tags, or product descriptions.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the core difference between content-based and collaborative recommendation?

options.text: Content-based matches item attributes; collaborative matches other users' behavior
Content-based requires thousands of users; collaborative works with just one
Content-based only works on text; collaborative only works on video

exercises.explain: Right! Content-based filtering looks at item features; collaborative filtering looks at user behavior patterns.

cards.title: The reader who only loves mysteries
cards.body: If you only ever read books tagged with "detective", "crime", and "mystery", a bookstore clerk doesn't need to ask anyone else what they like. They can just walk over to the shelf, find another book with those exact tags, and hand it to you. That's content-based filtering.

cards.title: Matching your profile to item features
cards.body: Content-based filtering works by building a user profile based on the features of items you've liked (e.g., 60% comedy, 40% sci-fi). It then calculates a similarity score between your profile and new items, suggesting the ones that match closest.

cards.title: A chef matching ingredients
cards.body: If a chef knows you love pasta with garlic, tomatoes, and basil, they can recommend a new dish made of tomatoes, garlic, and oregano. They aren't checking what other tables ordered; they are simply matching the ingredient profiles of the dishes.

cards.title: News feeds and online stores
cards.body: If you read three articles about technology on a news app, a content-based recommender notes your interest in the "Tech" tag and shows you more articles tagged "Tech". On a shopping site, if you buy a leather jacket, it recommends leather boots based on matching material attributes.

cards.title: The bubble trap of content-based filtering
cards.body: You might assume a recommender will eventually show you everything. But because content-based systems only suggest items similar to what you already liked, they can trap you in a "filter bubble." If you only watch sci-fi, it will never recommend a great documentary, because the attributes don't match your past history.

cards.title: How Content-Based Similarity Works
cards.body: Creating a match from user history to new item attributes:

left.text:
Liked: Movie A (Sci-Fi, Space)
Liked: Movie B (Sci-Fi, Action)

right.text:
Highly Recommended: Movie C (Sci-Fi, Space)
Low recommendation: Movie D (Romance, Comedy)

exercises.prompt: You watch three cooking videos on a video platform, and the app immediately suggests a fourth cooking video from a different creator. What kind of recommendation is this?

options.text: Content-based filtering, because it's matching the "cooking" category attribute of the videos
Collaborative filtering, because it's comparing you to other users

exercises.explain: Correct! Suggesting another video with the same topic/category attribute is content-based filtering.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Content-based filtering is highly likely to recommend a completely new genre of music that you have never listened to before.
exercises.explain: False! Because it matches your existing profile, content-based systems struggle to recommend items outside your historical preferences, often causing a filter bubble.

exercises.sentence: Content-based filtering matches new items to your user ___ based on features you previously liked.
exercises.explain: Your user profile acts as a summary of the item features (like genre, keywords, or authors) you have interacted with.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario is a content-based recommendation?

options.text: A job board suggests a software engineering listing because you previously clicked on three listings with the keyword "software engineer"
A job board suggests a marketing role because other software engineers also clicked on it
A job board displays listings in random order

exercises.explain: Correct! Matching the keyword attributes of past clicked jobs directly to a new listing is content-based filtering.

exercises.prompt: A streaming platform wants to launch a recommendation engine but has zero user accounts — there's only one user (you) on the site, and the platform has a rich database of movie metadata (genres, actors, directors). Which recommender type is viable?

options.text: Content-based filtering, because it only needs your personal watch history and item metadata to function, without needing other users' data
Collaborative filtering, because it runs perfectly with only one user
Neither, since all recommenders require at least 10,000 active users to start
Unsupervised clustering, because it requires no input features

exercises.explain: Exactly! Content-based filtering is ideal here because it doesn't need a crowd — it builds a profile from your history alone and matches it to item attributes.
